text: The surgeon closes the colostomy and introduces the mobilized colon into the abdominal cavity. A 12mm Husson balloon trocar is implanted into the previous colostomy opening.
Following diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon performs comprehensive adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions to achieve a tension-free colorectal anastomosis.

The surgeon examines a small bubble in the intestinal tract, confirming no further lesions of Crohn's disease. A segment of terminal ileum is identified for the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy.
The surgeon further mobilizes the colonic stump, detaching it from its surrounding attachments within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon evaluates the extension of the colonic stump into the pelvic region, assessing its position and alignment within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon releases the noma peritoneum, allowing the colonic stump to protrude visibly into the surgical field.
The surgeon seals the marginal artery and applies a first string suture clamp before removing the segment of colon leading toward the end colostomy.
The surgeon implants the anvil of a 31mm circular stapler into the colonic stem and secures it with a purse string suture.
The surgeon reintroduces the colon into the abdominal cavity and reimplants a twelve millimeter Hassan Ballantyre card.
The surgeon pierces the rectal stump with the shaft of a circular stapler, following dilation with upsizers from a 25mm to a 33mm diameter.
The surgeon creates an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis and performs a leak test to ensure the integrity of the connection.
The intraoperative flexible sigmoidoscopy reveals an intact anastomosis, confirming the successful connection between the colorectal segments.
The camera is adjusted to fit a 5mm trocar, and the terminal ileum segment is protruded through the colostomy site to establish a diverting loop ileostomy.
